elements.sent-2411-001: In contrast to 3DGS, we use the physical light model, which uses the connection between the camera and the light source.
elements.sent-2411-002: Moreover, we can reduce artifacts during novel view generation, which is a problem due to a limited camera trajectory during training.
elements.sent-2411-003: Finally, our model creates fewer artifacts than other state-of-the-art methods and responds to novel light conditions when we change the viewpoint or even modify geometry.
elements.sent-2411-004: Endoscopic procedures are crucial for colorectal cancer diagnosis, and three-dimensional reconstruction of the environment for real-time novel-view synthesis can significantly enhance diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present PR-ENDO, a framework that leverages 3D Gaussian Splatting within a physically based, relightable model tailored for the complex acquisition conditions in endoscopy, such as restricted camera rotations and strong view-dependent illumination.
elements.sent-2411-006: By exploiting the connection between the camera and light source, our approach introduces a relighting model to capture the intricate interactions between light and tissue using physically based rendering and MLP.
elements.sent-2411-007: Existing methods often produce artifacts and inconsistencies under these conditions, which PR-ENDO overcomes by incorporating a specialized diffuse MLP that utilizes light angles and normal vectors, achieving stable reconstructions even with limited training camera rotations.
elements.sent-2411-008: We benchmarked our framework using a publicly available dataset and a newly introduced dataset with wider camera rotations.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our methods demonstrated superior image quality compared to baseline approaches.
elements.sent-2411-010: Endoscopic images are crucial for the timely diagnosis of a wide range of pathological conditions, but their quality often suffers due to the challenging acquisition conditions \cite{pore2022colonoscopy .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Operating a flexible endoscope is complex due to the complex mapping between its tip and the non-ergonomic control interface, necessitating extensive training to master.
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite rigorous training, controlling the endoscope remains challenging and prone to human error, potentially causing patient discomfort and serious complications such as tissue perforation \cite{pore2022colonoscopy .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, inadequate control of the endoscope can result in incomplete colon inspections, as pathological areas may fall outside the field of view of the endoscopic video frames \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes, ma2021rnnslam, wang2024endogslam .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.80\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/prendo_qual.png} \caption{Qualitative comparison of \our{} and other methods such as Gaussian Pancakes and 3D Gaussian Splatting. Our method produces less artifacts and reconstructs geometry more accurately than other works. Please, zoom in for details.} \label{fig:c3vd}
elements.sent-2411-014: These limitations have motivated advancements aimed at enhancing colonoscopy techniques \cite{fu2021future-endonav .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Early approaches focused on automatically detecting suspicious pathological areas in endoscopic images \cite{barua2021ai-polyp-detect .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recent progress in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) within deformable environments has enabled tracking the endoscope tip's pose, thereby reconstructing a three-dimensional map of the colon \cite{mahmoud2017slam-mis, liu2022sage-slam, ma2021rnnslam .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite these developments, standard SLAM methods \cite{mur2015orb-slam,engel2017dso-slam, engel2014lsd-slam struggle with achieving dense reconstructions, limiting the quality of three-dimensional renderings.}
elements.sent-2411-018: High-quality, interactive 3D reconstructions of the colon can provide significant benefits, such as enabling endoscopists to review previously inspected areas in detail and synthesize new views for optimal diagnostic evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, accurate colon reconstructions could create realistic, interactive environments for training doctors, with models that can respond to natural body movements and simulate realistic relighting effects.
elements.sent-2411-020: In such settings, an animatable and responsive 3D colon model could be invaluable for both diagnostic and educational purposes, allowing future practitioners to practice in environments derived from real patient data.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recent developments in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) \cite{mildenhall2021nerf and Neural Implicit Surface (NeuS) \cite{wang2021neus have been applied to colonoscopy for novel view synthesis \cite{shi2023colonnerf, batlle2023lightneus, psychogyios2023REIM-NeRF .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, these approaches are hindered by long training times, slow and non-interactive volumetric rendering with the absence of an explicit geometric model, limiting their clinical applicability.
elements.sent-2411-023: These challenges have been addressed by introducing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) \cite{kerbl20233dgs , which models the environment using a set of Gaussians.}
elements.sent-2411-024: While 3DGS offers fast training and real-time rendering, it relies on the quality of the explicit geometric model of the environment, typically reconstructed using SLAM techniques in endoscopic settings \cite{ma2021rnnslam, wang2024endogslam .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The work in \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes presents a 3DGS approach that incorporates geometric regularization with a dense SLAM technique but does not account for the illumination model or the ability to apply movements to the reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this work, we address these limitations by proposing \our{}\footnote{Code and dataset are available at: \url{https://github.com/SanoScience/PR-ENDO .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Our model is specifically designed to handle the complex conditions of endoscopic acquisitions, characterized by a camera with a limited range of motion, primarily moving forward with minimal changes in orientation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, we consider that the flexible endoscope integrates the camera and light source in a narrow space, effectively creating a single point of view for each specific illumination pattern.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our physically based illumination framework produces a physically motivated 3D representation, enabling the generation of new views with larger camera rotation angles, improved generalization, and flexibility in adapting the 3DGS reconstruction to simulate anatomical or endoscopic movements.
elements.sent-2411-030: As noted in \cite{sss_gs , implicit representation of complex effects like subsurface scattering is the most efficient approach.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Therefore, our method extends the basic coefficient-based model by incorporating additional components to capture lighting effects that simpler models may overlook.
elements.sent-2411-032: We experimentally evaluate the proposed framework using a set of metrics that assess the quality and speed of rendering new views, comparing it with the most relevant publicly available methods in the state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-033: The evaluation is based on two distinct datasets: one public and the other introduced in this work to provide a larger and more challenging set of camera rotations.
elements.sent-2411-034: In summary, the contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-035: A relightning model based on light and tissue properties in the specific context of endoscopy, capable of separating the effects of illumination on the tissue model.
elements.sent-2411-036: The introduction of diffuseMLP, which synthesizes new views while minimizing artifacts and visual inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-037: Demonstrating that the proposed model can handle wider camera angles and modify the scene model to replicate anatomical movements.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/method.png} \caption{\textbf{Overview of \our{}} pipeline, which includes: (1) Initialization: SLAM module generates camera poses, point cloud and optionally depth maps. (2) Scene Parameters: Each Gaussian \( i \) is defined by position, covariance \( \Sigma_{i} \), opacity, albedo \( a_{i} \), roughness \( r_{i} \), and reflectivity \( F0_{_{i}} \). Light parameters include direction \( \mathbf{l} \), intensity \(I\) and attenuation function \( att(d_{i}) \). (3) Prediction: \(\ourMLP{} \), predicts the diffuse component, while the PBR model calculates specular reflection. The final color \( \mathrm{RGB}_{\mathrm{relighted}} \) combines diffuse and specular terms. (4) Supervision: Normal, reconstruction, and physical losses are applied during training. } \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-038: Novel View Synthesis Synthesizing new views is an essential step in modern 3D reconstruction pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-039: Many supervised approaches have been proposed, exploiting different efficient representations, in particular meshes \cite{guo2015robust-mesh,collet2015high-mesh , voxels \cite{hu2022hvtr-20-4dgs, guo2019relightables-18-4dgs and hybrid methods \cite{guo2023vmesh-hybrid, broxton2020immersive-hybrid .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: NeRF-based approaches demonstrate that implicit radiance fields can efficiently learn scene representations and synthesize high-quality new views \cite{barron2021mip-nerf,mildenhall2021nerf,zhang2020nerf++ .}
elements.sent-2411-041: NeRF learns a continuous function that implicitly represents the 3D scene trained from 2D images and matched camera poses.
elements.sent-2411-042: NeRF is an inefficient approach, requiring long training times and unable to achieve real-time rendering of new views.
elements.sent-2411-043: These limitations have been recently overcome by 3DGS approaches, which propose an efficient representation of the scene via a set of 3D Gaussians \cite{kerbl20233dgs .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In our work, we, therefore, adopt a 3DGS approach, since its characteristics make it ideal for synthesizing new views in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-045: SLAM \& Novel View Synthesis in Endoscopy NeRF and 3DGS approaches require knowledge of the camera poses and a three-dimensional reconstruction of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-046: This information is not commonly available in the endoscopic context, and must be reconstructed with specific approaches, usually exploiting a SLAM approach \cite{mur2015orb-slam, engel2014lsd-slam, engel2017dso-slam, liu2022sage-slam .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Unfortunately, deformations of the environment, poorly defined textures, and constrained and limited camera movements are very complex conditions for the use of standard SLAM approaches \cite{mahmoud2017slam-mis .}
elements.sent-2411-048: In this context, approaches designed explicitly for the endoscopic context have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-049: In particular, RNNSLAM proposes the extension of the standard SLAM approach with two recurrent neural networks for pose estimation and depth maps to improve the quality of the environment reconstruction and also the tracking of camera movements \cite{ma2021rnnslam .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Similarly, ColonNeRF proposes a specifically developed pipeline able to refine the poses and the environment reconstruction to support the subsequent NeRF modeling \cite{shi2023colonnerf .}
elements.sent-2411-051: EndoGSLAM has recently proposed an efficient approach that integrates a SLAM approach with a 3DGS reconstruction \cite{wang2024endogslam .}
elements.sent-2411-052: In this work, we adopt EndoGSLAM to obtain the camera pose and the 3DGS reconstruction of the environment, which we extend with respect to the simplified model adopted in the original work, in particular to model the effect of view-dependent illumination.
elements.sent-2411-053: However, our method is universal for other types of initialization and can be used with different SLAM modules.
elements.sent-2411-054: The reconstruction approaches described, both NeRF and 3DGS, suffer in the endoscopic context from the limited camera movements and from the fact that the light source moves together with the camera generating a highly view-dependent illumination, which makes it difficult to synthesize new high-quality views, i.e. free from inconsistencies and artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-055: To improve the quality of the synthesis, Gaussian Pancakes extends the RNNSLAM approach to extract a 3DGS reconstruction on which geometric and depth regularization terms are introduced in order to improve the geometric quality of the reconstruction and consequently that of the images synthesized in correspondence with the new views \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Gaussian Pancakes does not consider the illumination model, which is instead done by REIM-NeRF, which trains a light source location-conditioned NeRF model \cite{psychogyios2023REIM-NeRF .}
elements.sent-2411-057: This approach suffers from the inherent inefficiencies of the NeRF model and considers a lighting model that neglects the environmental components, which are instead considered in the proposed PR-ENDO approach.
elements.sent-2411-058: In its original formulation 3DGS uses a single spherical harmonic function to model both texture and color information, reducing the generalization capabilities to new views.
elements.sent-2411-059: Various methods have been proposed to indirectly include light effects \cite{malarz2024gaussiansplattingnerfbasedcolor or decouple texture information from illumination, exploiting forward \cite{kaleta2024lumigausshighfidelityoutdoorrelighting,liang2024gs-ir, jiang2024gaussianshader, gao2023relightable-3dgs, shi2023gir or deferred shading \cite{wu2024deferred-gs, chen2024gi-gs to achieve effective Gaussian relighting.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: GS-IR uses baking-based volumes to store occlusion and indirect illumination \cite{liang2024gs-ir .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Relightable 3D Gaussian achieves this by employing point-based ray tracing to bake occlusion and parameterizing indirect lighting with additional spherical harmonics for each Gaussian \cite{gao2023relightable-3dgs .}
elements.sent-2411-062: GI-GS utilizes deferred shading to enable efficient path tracing-based occlusion and indirect illumination for rendering and relighting \cite{chen2024gi-gs , a feature not supported by previous methods.}
elements.sent-2411-063: GaussianShader introduces a shading function to 3DGS and a normal estimation framework capable of handling reflective surfaces \cite{jiang2024gaussianshader , enhancing the approach proposed in GIR \cite{shi2023gir .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Together with DeferredGS \cite{wu2024deferred-gs , GaussianShader extends 3DGS to the inverse rendering task by associating BRDFs with Gaussians.}
elements.sent-2411-065: GS3 proposes a triple splatting process that does not require robust normal estimation and accurately models BRDF properties using a mixture of angular Gaussians \cite{bi2024gs3 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: First, we present the preliminaries on vanilla Gaussian Splatting algorithm \cite{kerbl20233dgs and physically based rendering.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Then, we present \our{ , which combines Gaussian Splatting with a relighting model, dedicated for endoscopy data.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Each i-th Gaussian is defined by a mean position \( xyz_i \) and a covariance matrix \( \Sigma_i \), representing the spatial distribution in 3D.
elements.sent-2411-070: Additionally, each Gaussian is assigned an opacity \( \sigma_i \) and a view-dependent color \( c_i \).
elements.sent-2411-071: The covariance matrix \( \Sigma_i \) is derived from a scaling matrix \( S_i \) and a rotation matrix \( R_i \) through the relationship $ \Sigma_i = R_i S_i S_i^T R_i^T.
elements.sent-2411-072: The covariance matrix in camera coordinates is computed as: \begin{equation}\Sigma_i' = J W \Sigma_i W^T J^T,\end{equation} where \( W \) is the viewing transformation matrix, and \( J \) is the Jacobian matrix of the affine approximation of \( W \).
elements.sent-2411-073: Rendering is performed through a differentiable point-based alpha blending technique, where the color of each pixel \( C \) is accumulated along the ray by blending ordered 2D Gaussians from front to back: \begin{equation}C = \sum_{i \in N} T_i \alpha_i c_i, \quad T_i = \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} (1 - \alpha_j).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: Here, \( \alpha \) is computed by combining the opacity \( \sigma \) with contributions from the 2D covariance matrix \( \Sigma' \) and the pixel location in image space.
elements.sent-2411-075: The attributes of each Gaussian, including color \( c_i \), are encoded with spherical harmonics (SH) to capture view-dependent effects \cite{ramamoorthi2001efficient .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/colonrotate.png} \caption{Qualitative comparison of \our{} and other methods such as Gaussian Pancakes and 3D Gaussian Splatting. A comparison was made with our dataset ColonRotate, which has ground truth renders for various camera rotation angles.} \label{fig:colonrotate}
elements.sent-2411-076: Physically-Based Rendering (PBR) \cite{disney2012pbr, pbrbook simulates light-material interactions based on physical principles, enabling realistic image synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Material properties in PBR govern how surfaces interact with light, typically modeled through diffuse and specular reflections.
elements.sent-2411-078: The Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) is defined as the sum of the diffuse and specular reflection terms: \begin{equation}\text{BRDF} = k_d f_{\text{diffuse}} + f_{\text{specular}}\end{equation} where \( k_d \) is the diffuse reflection coefficient, and \( f_{\text{diffuse \) is the diffuse reflection term, \( f_{\text{specular \) is the specular reflection term.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The latter term includes the specular reflection coefficient $k_s$, which is related to $k_d$ by $ k_d = 1 - k_s.$ Diffuse Component The diffuse term represents the scattering of light uniformly in all directions on a rough surface.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is typically modeled using the Lambertian reflectance model, which defines the diffuse reflection as: \begin{equation}f_{\text{diffuse}} = a \cdot (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l})\end{equation} where: - \( \mathbf{n \) is the surface normal, - \( \mathbf{l \) is the light direction, - \( a \) is the diffuse albedo (reflectivity of the material in diffuse reflections).}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Specular Component Specular reflection models mirror-like reflections influenced by view angle and surface roughness.
elements.sent-2411-082: In PBR, the Cook-Torrance model computes the specular term as: \begin{equation}f_{\text{specular}} = \frac{D \, G \, F}{4 \, (\mathbf{l} \cdot \mathbf{n})(\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{n})} \label{eq:pbr_full}\end{equation} where: \( \mathbf{c \) is the viewing (camera) direction, \( D \) is the microfacet distribution function, describing how microfacets are oriented on the surface, \( G \) is the geometric attenuation term, accounting for shadowing and masking between microfacets, \( F \) is the Fresnel term, representing the angle-dependent reflectivity of the surface.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/normal_comp.png} \caption{\textbf{Normal comparison between \our{} and 3DGS-DR \cite{wu2024deferred-gs}.} Although both methods produce plausible renderings, our method has a superior normals reconstruction.} \label{fig:normal_comparison}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/decomposition_small.png} \caption{Decomposition. Thanks to our physically motivated model, we are able to decompose the render into diffuse, normals and albedo views.} \label{fig:decompose}
elements.sent-2411-083: Fresnel Effect The Fresnel effect describes how reflectivity changes with the viewing angle, especially pronounced at grazing angles.
elements.sent-2411-084: It is computed as: \begin{equation}F = F_0 + (1 - F_0) (1 - (\mathbf{l} \cdot \mathbf{H}))^5\end{equation} where \( F_0 \) is the base reflectivity at normal incidence, \( \mathbf{H \) is the half-vector between the light direction \( \mathbf{l \) and view direction \( \mathbf{c \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Microfacet Distribution The microfacet distribution function \( D \) models surface roughness, commonly using the Trowbridge-Reitz GGX distribution: \begin{equation}D = \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi \, ((\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{H})^2 (\alpha^2 - 1) + 1)^2}\end{equation} where \( \alpha = \text{roughness ^2 \), representing the surface roughness.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The GGX distribution captures both sharp and rough reflections, making it versatile for a range of materials.
elements.sent-2411-087: Here, \( \mathbf{H \) is the half-vector, and \( \mathbf{n \cdot \mathbf{H \) represents the angle between the surface normal and the half-vector.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Geometric Attenuation The geometric attenuation term \( G \) accounts for shadowing and masking effects on microfacets, which reduces the amount of light reflected due to self-shadowing on rough surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-089: The Schlick-Beckmann approximation simplifies this calculation: \begin{equation}G = \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l}}{(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l})(1 - k) + k} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{c}}{(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{c})(1 - k) + k}\end{equation} where \( k = \frac{\alpha {2 \).}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}G = \frac{n \cdot l}{(n \cdot l)(1 - k) + k} \cdot \frac{n \cdot c}{(n \cdot c)(1 - k) + k}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-090: This approximation efficiently models the effects of surface roughness on reflection attenuation, especially when combined with the GGX distribution.
elements.sent-2411-091: Our method, \our{ , introduces a 3DGS physically-based relighting model tailored for endoscopic scenes, capturing both diffuse and specular interactions between light and surfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-092: We show the overview of our method in \cref{fig:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-093: We use a dedicated light model, which assumes that the light source and camera are co-located.
elements.sent-2411-094: Endoscopy videos are characterized by limited training views due to specific and challenging training camera trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, generalization to novel viewpoints (such as previously unseen rotations) leads to artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-096: To tackle this problem, instead of simple coefficient-based diffuse relightning model, we introduce \ourMLP{ \( \theta \).}
elements.sent-2411-097: The MLP is fed with Gaussian normal vector \( \mathbf{n_i \) and light position parameters (\( \mathbf{l_i \),\(d_i\)) relative to the i-th Gaussian.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: We add small random noise to the inputs to improve robustness in different lighting and viewpoint variations, encouraging consistent diffuse predictions under similar conditions.
elements.sent-2411-099: The output returns diffuse part of the i-th Gaussian color: \begin{equation}f_{\text{diffuse}, i} = \theta(\mathbf{l_i},d_i, \mathbf{n_i})\end{equation} The result of \ourMLP{ not only allows for better generalization to novel rotations but can also incorporate additional lightning effects not included in the simple coefficient-based diffuse model.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The specular component \(f_{specular, i}\) for the i-th Gaussian is computed using the PBR model as in \cref{eq:pbr_full , based on \(r_{i}\) and \(F0_{i}\).}
elements.sent-2411-101: In our method we clamp $F_0$ values to 0.03, which represent realistic values of the colon refractive index \cite{refractive .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The final relighted color, \( RGB_{\text{relight , i \) is obtained by combining the diffuse and specular components, scaled by the base color \(a_i\), light intensity \( I \) and a distance-based attenuation factor \( att(d_i) \): \begin{equation}\begin{split} RGB_{\mathrm{relight}, i} = I \cdot att(d_{i}) \cdot \left( f_{\text{diffuse}, i} \cdot (1 - F_{i}) \cdot a_{i} + f_{\text{specular}, i} \right) \end{split}\end{equation} In our data-driven PBR model, all material, as well as light properties, are optimized via gradient descent.}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} RGB_{relight, i} = I \cdot att(d_{i}) \cdot \left( f_{diffuse, i} \cdot (1 - F_{i}) \cdot a_{i} + f_{specular, i} \right) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-103: Our model's total loss function \( \mathcal{L \) combines image reconstruction, depth, geometric, diffuse, and tissue consistency losses, designed to optimize visual fidelity, depth alignment, and physical realism.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Image Loss: An \(L_1\) loss, \(\mathcal{L _{\text{Image }\), measures the pixel-wise difference between rendered and ground truth images.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Structural Similarity Loss: A structural similarity index (SSIM) loss, \(\mathcal{L _{\text{D-SSIM }\), compares the rendered and ground truth images, balancing pixel accuracy with perceived similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Depth Loss: To address the minimal depth diversity in endoscopic images, we apply a depth loss based on the Huber loss function following \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes .}
elements.sent-2411-107: For depth maps \(\{ D_i \}_{i=1 ^M\), where \( M \) is the number of viewpoints, the depth loss \(\mathcal{L _D\) is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_D(i) = \begin{cases} 0.5 \Delta D_i^2, \text{if } |\Delta D_i| < \delta \delta \left(|\Delta D_i| - 0.5 \delta \right), \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\end{equation} where \( \Delta D_i = |D_i - \hat{D _i| \) denotes the difference between true and rendered depth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Geometric Regularization: To enforce structural consistency, a geometric loss \(\mathcal{L _G\) \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes is applied, aligning Gaussians with the surface's principal curvature.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The cosine similarity-based geometric loss is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_G = 1 - \frac{ \left| A \cdot B \right| }{ \| A \| \| B \|}\end{equation} where \( A \) and \( B \) are the normal vectors of nearest neighbors on the surface.
elements.sent-2411-110: The overall incorporated loss function \( \mathcal{L _{\text{inc \) is a weighted sum: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{inc}} = (1 - \lambda_1) \mathcal{L}_{\text{Image}} + \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{D-SSIM}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_D + \lambda_3 \mathcal{L}_G.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-112: Diffuse Loss: To ensure consistency between the diffuse lighting predicted by the \ourMLP{ \(f_{\text{diffuse , \text{MLP , i}\) and the coefficients \(f_{\text{diffuse , \text{coeff , i}\), we apply a mean squared error loss summed over all (\(N\)) Gaussians: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{Diffuse}} = \sum_{i=1}^N \left(f_{\text{diffuse}, \text{MLP}, i} - f_{\text{diffuse}, \text{coeff}, i}\right)^2\end{equation} Tissue Consistency Loss: To enforce consistency in tissue characteristics, we constrain the albedo \(a\), roughness \(r\) and base reflectance \(F0\) to remain close to their respective means with each component measured as an MSE and summed over all (\(N\)) Gaussians: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\text{Tissue}} = \sum_{i=1}^N (a_{i} - a_{\text{mean}})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^N (r_{i} - r_{\text{mean}})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^N (F0_i - F0_{ \text{mean}})^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Each part of the equation can be thought as a separate loss for each component respectively (\(\mathcal{L _\text{a}\), \(\mathcal{L _\text{r}\), \(\mathcal{L _\text{F0}\)).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The final loss function \( \mathcal{L \) is the sum of all loss terms, balanced with appropriate weights: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{inc}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{Diffuse}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{Tissue}}\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-174: Endoluminal endoscopic procedures are essential for diagnosing colorectal cancer and other severe conditions in the digestive tract, urogenital system, and airways.
elements.sent-2411-175: Moreover, integrating physiological deformations and interaction with the endoscope enables the development of simulation tools from real video data.

elements.sent-2411-177: We present \our{ , a novel 3D reconstruction framework leveraging the unique property of endoscopic imaging, where a single light source is closely aligned with the camera.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Our method separates light effects from tissue properties.
elements.sent-2411-179: We boost the traditional light transport formulation with a specialized MLP capturing complex light-related effects while ensuring reduced artifacts and better generalization across novel views.
elements.sent-2411-180: Unlike existing approaches, \our{ enables tissue modifications while preserving a physically accurate response to light, making it closer to real-world clinical use.. Repository: \url{https://github.com/SanoScience/PR-ENDO .}}
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img/method.jpg} \caption{\textbf{Overview of \our{}} pipeline: (1) Initialization: SLAM module generates camera poses and point cloud and optionally depth maps. (2) Scene Parameters: Each Gaussian \( i \) is defined by position, covariance \( \Sigma_{i} \), opacity \( \alpha_{i} \), albedo \( a_{i} \), roughness \( r_{i} \), and reflectivity \( F_{0,i} \). Light parameters include direction \( \mathbf{l} \), intensity \(I\) and attenuation coefficients for \( att \). (3) Prediction: \(\ourMLP{} \) predicts the diffuse component, while the PBR model calculates specular reflection. The final color \( \mathrm{RGB}_{\mathrm{relight}} \) combines diffuse and specular terms. (4) Supervision: Normal, reconstruction and physical losses are applied during training. } \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-181: Endoluminal Endoscopy (EE) is a minimally invasive technique widely used for diagnosing and treating complex conditions, including tumors, within luminal structures such as the airways, digestive tract, and urogenital system \cite{pore2023autonomous .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Despite its clinical significance, EE remains technically challenging due to the complexity of maneuvering a flexible endoscope in constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-183: These difficulties can lead to incomplete inspections and risks to patient safety \cite{pore2023autonomous .}
elements.sent-2411-184: High-fidelity, interactive 3D reconstructions of luminal structures offer a powerful solution by allowing clinicians to revisit previously examined areas, synthesize novel views, and improve diagnostic accuracy \cite{ma2021rnnslam .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Additionally, accurate lumen reconstructions facilitate realistic, patient-specific training environments \cite{dlInEndoReview and provide valuable data for developing autonomous robotic platforms \cite{roboBiopsy , \cite{pore2023autonomous .}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Recent advances in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) \cite{mildenhall2021nerf and Neural Implicit Surfaces (NeuS) \cite{wang2021neus have been applied to EE, particularly in colonoscopy, for novel view synthesis \cite{shi2023colonnerf , \cite{batlle2023lightneus , \cite{psychogyios2023REIM-NeRF .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: However, these methods are computationally expensive, require long training and rendering times and are hindered by non-explicit, difficult to edit representation.
elements.sent-2411-188: Several studies have explored 3DGS for endoscopic reconstruction \cite{endosurf , \cite{wang2022neural , \cite{Li_EndoSparse_MICCAI2024 , \cite{Hua_Endo4DGS_MICCAI2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Especially for EE, \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes enhances 3DGS with geometric and depth regularization, while \cite{wang2024endogslam jointly estimates camera poses and reconstruction.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: However, none of these works address the challenges posed by endoscopic lighting.
elements.sent-2411-191: As a result, illumination effects are typically baked into textures or mimicked by "floaters", leading to inaccurate reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-192: Inverse rendering techniques have been developed to enhance 3DGS with separation of lighting effects from object material properties \cite{jiang2024gaussianshader , \cite{ye2024gsdr , \cite{chen2024gi-gs , \cite{gao2023relightable , \cite{liang2024gs-ir , \cite{sss_gs , \cite{kaleta2025lumigauss .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Most approaches represent materials with the use of Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF).
elements.sent-2411-194: Malarz et al. \cite{malarz2024gaussiansplattingnerfbasedcolor model camera-dependent effects but it lacks physical interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-195: None of these works specifically consider EE challenges, such as very constrained camera view with a strong view dependent illumination.
elements.sent-2411-196: Moreover, none have explored a physically correct editable model, a crucial feature for medical simulation.
elements.sent-2411-197: In this work, we propose \our{ , a 3D reconstruction framework that leverages the co-location of the endoscopy camera and the light source.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Built on 3DGS, our method offers fast training and rendering while separating illumination effects from tissue properties.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our approach enables accurate re-illumination of luminal structures, including cases where tissue properties or geometry are arbitrarily modified, making it highly applicable to real-world clinical use.
elements.sent-2411-200: We evaluate our framework on two datasets: one public \cite{bobrow2023c3vd and another introduced in this work to provide a more diverse and challenging set of test camera rotations.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: (1) A 3D relightable reconstruction framework that models interactions between tissue and endoscopic light source in a physically correct way.

elements.sent-2411-202: First, we present the preliminaries on vanilla 3DGS algorithm \cite{kerbl20233dgs and physically based rendering.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Then, we present \our{ , which combines 3DGS with a relighting model, dedicated to EE data.}
elements.sent-2411-204: It represents the scene as a collection of 3D Gaussian primitives, each characterized by a mean position, a covariance matrix encoding spatial uncertainty, an opacity term, and a view-dependent color.
elements.sent-2411-205: The covariance matrix is parameterized using a scaling matrix \( S_i \) and a rotation matrix \( R_i \), forming: \begin{equation}\Sigma_i = R_i S_i S_i^T R_i^T.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-206: Rendering projects 3D Gaussians onto a 2D image plane by transforming their covariance matrix in screen space: \begin{equation}\Sigma_i' = J W \Sigma_i W^T J^T,\end{equation} where \( W \) is the viewing transformation, and \( J \) accounts for perspective distortion.
elements.sent-2411-207: The final image is synthesized via differentiable alpha blending, with each Gaussian contributing based on opacity and position.
elements.sent-2411-208: The accumulated pixel color is: \begin{equation}C = \textstyle\sum_{i \in N} T_i \alpha_i c_i, \quad T_i = \prod_{j=1}^{i-1} (1 - \alpha_j),\end{equation} where \( \alpha_i \) is per-Gaussian opacity, modulated by its transformed covariance.
elements.sent-2411-209: For exact derivation and further details on the formulation and rendering process, we refer to \cite{kerbl20233dgs .}

elements.sent-2411-212: To enable physically-based relighting of 3D Gaussians we associate each Gaussian with BRDF properties.
elements.sent-2411-213: Surface reflectance follows the BRDF with Cook-Torrance microfacet model \cite{cooktorr : \begin{equation}\text{BRDF} = k_d f_{\text{diffuse}} + k_s f_{\text{specular}}\end{equation} where \( k_d \) and \( k_s \) controls the diffuse and specular contribution respectively and sum to 1.0.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The diffuse term \( f_{\text{diffuse \) is proportional to albedo.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: The specular term \( f_{\text{specular \) depends on light and view directions and is formulated as: \begin{equation}f_{\text{specular}} = \frac{D(h, r) \cdot F(\mathbf{c}, h, F_0) \cdot G(\mathbf{l}, \mathbf{c}, h, r)} {4 \cdot(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l}) \cdot (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{c})}, \label{eq:specular_term}\end{equation} where \( r \) is the roughness, \( F_0 \) is the base reflectance, \( \mathbf{l \) is the light direction, \( \mathbf{c \) is the view (camera) direction, \( h = (\mathbf{l + \mathbf{c )/2 \) is the half-vector and \( \mathbf{n \) is the surface normal.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}f_{specular} = \frac{D(h, r) \cdot F(c, h, F_0) \cdot G(l, c, h, r)} {4 \cdot(n \cdot l) \cdot (n \cdot c)}, \label{eq:specular_term}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-216: The terms \( D \), \( F \), and \( G \) correspond to the microfacet distribution function, Fresnel effect, and geometry term, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-217: We employ the Trowbridge-Reitz GGX distribution for \( D \), Schlick's approximation for \( F \), and the Schlick-Beckmann model for \( G \).
elements.sent-2411-218: For further details, we refer to \cite{cooktorr , \cite{disney2012pbr .}}

elements.sent-2411-228: The final relit color, \( RGB_{\text{relight , i \), is obtained by combining the diffuse and specular components, scaled by light intensity \( I \), distance-based attenuation \( \text{att (d_i) \), and dot product between light direction and Gaussian normal: \begin{equation}RGB_{\mathrm{relight}, i} = I \cdot att(d_{i}) \cdot \left( f_{\text{diffuseMLP}, i} \cdot (1 - F_{i}) + f_{\text{specular}, i} \right) \cdot (\boldsymbol{n}_i \cdot \boldsymbol{l}_i)\end{equation} To enhance the effect of light position, we optionally encode the light position using HashGrid (HG), a multi-resolution hash table, as an additional input to \ourMLP{ .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-231: Our total loss function \( \mathcal{L \) incorporates multiple terms from prior work to ensure fidelity, depth alignment, and structural consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Specifically, we adopt the image reconstruction loss \( \mathcal{L _{\text{RGB \) and SSIM loss \( \mathcal{L _{\text{D-SSIM \) for perceptual quality, depth loss \( \mathcal{L _{\text{Depth \) from \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes to handle minimal depth variation, and normal loss \( \mathcal{L _{\text{Norm \) from \cite{bonilla2024gaussianpancakes to enforce normal consistency.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: We introduce two novel losses: Diffuse Loss: Ensures consistency between MLP-based irradiance and irradiance computed for point light using classic light transport equation: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{Diffuse}} = \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^N \left(f_{\text{diffuseMLP}, i} - f_{\text{diffuse,ClassicEq}, i}\right)^2\end{equation} Tissue Consistency Loss: Regularizes albedo, roughness, and base reflectance across Gaussians: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{Tissue}} = \textstyle\sum_{i=1}^N (a_{i} - a_{\text{mean}})^2 + (r_{i} - r_{\text{mean}})^2 + (F_{0,i} - F_{0,\text{mean}})^2\end{equation} The final objective function is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{RGB}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{D-SSIM}} +\mathcal{L}_{\text{Depth}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{Norm}} +\mathcal{L}_{\text{Diffuse}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{Tissue}}\end{equation}.

elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth, trim=0 0 0 0, clip]{img_appendix/teaser.jpg} \caption{PR-ENDO adapts classical Gaussian Splatting for endoscopy environments. In contrast to 3DGS, we use a physical light model that incorporates the connection between the camera and the light source. Moreover, we reduce artifacts during novel view generation—a common issue due to limited camera trajectory during training. Finally, our model generates fewer artifacts than other state-of-the-art methods and responds effectively to novel lighting conditions when the viewpoint is changed or the geometry is modified.} \label{fig:teaser}
